name: Samarium Sm-153 lexidronam pentasodium
description: Samarium Sm-153 lexidronam pentasodium, also known as Quadramet, is a chelated complex of a samarium radioisotope with a tetraphosphate chelator, ethylenediaminetetramethylenephosphonic acid (EDTMP), used to treat cancer pain associated with bone mets. The exact mechanism of action for how it reduces pain is unknown but studies have shown that the drug is quickly cleared from the blood and localises in the osteoid layer undergoing mineralization. Pain relief is normally felt within 1 week of administration and may last for 4 months or longer. It should be stored frozen in a lead shielded container until it is to be used and is good for 8 hours after thawing or 56 hours after the time of calibration noted on the label, whichever is earlier. The medication is safe in the elderly but has not been studied in renal failure.
indication: It is indicated for the relief of bone pain in patients with multiple osteoblastc skeletal metastases which have been confirmed by positive radionuclide bone scan.
toxicity: No overdoses have been reported however it is suspected complications would likely be secondary to bone marrow suppression due to the radioactivity of the drug and/or hypocalcemia and cardiac arrhythmia's due to the EDTMP. The LD50 values of the EDTMP component in mice and rats are 300 fold higher then the recommended dosing for treatment in humans.
mechanismOfAction: The exact mechanism of action for how it reduces pain is unknown.
pharmacodynamics: Samarium Sm-153 lexidronam pentasodium works through an unknown mechanism to relive bone pain in patients with osteoblastic skeletal metastases. It has an affinity for skeletal tissue and accumulates in areas of bone turnover in association with hydroxyapatite. Studies in rats have shown that it accumulates in areas of growing bone matrix and clinical studies have shown that metastatic areas can accumulate more of the drug then surrounding normal bone.
